children.text: Eightieth session – Agenda item 177 – Financing of the United Nations Support Office in Haiti – Proposed budgets for the periods from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 and from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 for the United Nations Support Office in Haiti – Report of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions

table_content.text: Proposal submitted by the Secretary-General for 2025/26
Adjustment recommended by the Advisory Committee for 2025/26
Recommendation of the Advisory Committee for 2025/26
Proposal submitted by the Secretary-General for 2026/27
Adjustment recommended by the Advisory Committee for 2026/27
Recommendation of the Advisory Committee for 2026/27

a	Commitment authority for the period 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 as per General Assembly resolution 80/241, including $54,163,500 (plus the equivalent of $50 million from strategic deployment stocks) for the period from 1 October to 31 December 2025 under the authority of the Advisory Committee (AC 2358).
	b	A total amount of $270,004,500 has been assessed on Member States.
	c	Estimates as at 31 March 2026.

a	Commitment authority for the period 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 as per General Assembly resolution 80/241, including $54,163,500 (plus the equivalent of $50 million from strategic deployment stocks) for the period from 1 October to 31 December 2025 under the authority of the Advisory Committee (AC 2358).
	b	A total amount of $270,004,500 has been assessed on Member States.
	c	Estimates as at 31 March 2026.

children.text: 1.	During its consideration of an advance version of the report of the Secretary-General on the budgets for the periods from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 and from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 for the United Nations Support Office in Haiti (UNSOH), the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions met with representatives of the Secretary-General, who provided additional information and clarification, concluding with written responses on 7 April 2026. The comments and recommendations of the Committee on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations are contained in its report (A/80/604). As the operations of UNSOH commenced during the third quarter of 2025, the Board of Auditors have not yet reviewed UNSOH as part of its report on the United Nations peacekeeping operations for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 (A/80/633).

2.	The mandate and planning assumptions regarding UNSOH are described in detail in section I of the report of the Secretary-General. The Security Council, by its resolution 2793 (2025) of 30 September 2025, acting under Chapter VII of the Charter of the United Nations, authorized Member States to transition the Multinational Security Support Mission to the Gang Suppression Force, with an authorized ceiling of 5,550 personnel (5,500 uniformed military and police personnel and 50 civilians). As further detailed in the resolution, UNSOH would, inter alia, provide: (a) logistical support to the Gang Suppression Force, funded from assessed contributions; (b) the standard range of mission support services to the United Nations Integrated Office in Haiti (BINUH), on a cost-recovery basis and with a view to assume full responsibility by 1 February 2026; and (c) in-theatre medical evacuation for the Haitian National Police and Haitian Armed Forces on joint operations with the Gang Suppression Force, on an exceptional and cost-recovery basis.

3.	In its resolution 2793 (2025), the Security Council also indicates that the Standing Group of Partners for the Gang Suppression Force is responsible for the selection of the special representative for the Force, who was appointed in December 2025, to provide strategic representation and coordination and also of the Force Commander of the Force, to be responsible for operational command and day-to-day operational decision-making. The Advisory Committee recalls that the Standing Group of Partners and the Gang Suppression Force would be responsible for the recruitment of their own uniformed and civilian personnel and that the related personnel costs would be funded through voluntary contributions from the trust fund of the Multinational Security Support Mission (see para. 7 below).

4.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the Standing Group of Partners had received pledges for over 11,000 uniformed personnel for the Gang Suppression Force, in support of the mandated strength of 5,500 personnel, and that a total of 1,000 personnel were currently deployed in Port-au-Prince. The Committee was informed that the Department of Operational Support was negotiating memorandums of understanding for multiple units of the Gang Suppression Force; as at 3 March 2026, no memorandums had been signed. The Advisory Committee notes that the actual level of deployment, as at 31 March 2026, has been delayed in comparison with the planned deployment estimated at the time of presenting the request for a commitment authority in 2025 and trusts that an update on actual deployment levels and the negotiations for the deployment of additional uniformed personnel, as well as on the status of the memorandums of understanding with troop- and police-contributing countries, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.

5.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the Gang Suppression Force would establish regional headquarters, with a significant presence at two locations that are seen as critical areas for the control of cross-border operations. This would represent an important step forwards as it would indicate that the situation in Port-au-Prince had improved to some extent. The Committee was also informed that the concept of operations for the Gang Suppression Force was being finalized. The Advisory Committee notes the concept of operations is still being finalized and trusts that updated and more detailed information thereon will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of consideration of the present report and in the next report. The Committee also notes the planned presence of the Force in two locations in Haiti other than Port-au-Prince, while also highlighting that this would be an important step indicating improvement in the situation in the capital. The Committee trusts that more clarity on the plans and timelines for the Gang Suppression Force to establish its presence outside of Port-au-Prince, and any conditions that are expected to guide this expansion of operations, should be provided to the Assembly at the time of consideration of the present report and in the next report.

6.	In its resolution 2793 (2025), the Security Council requested the Secretary-General to ensure strong coordination and cooperation among all actors in the United Nations system in order to prevent duplication of effort and to maximize the leveraging of existing resources, and stressed that the Head of UNSOH should have separate quantifiable compacts with the Head of BINUH for the delivery of support to BINUH and with the special representative for the Gang Suppression Force. The Advisory Committee was informed that discussions between the three entities were ongoing regarding a coordination mechanism on the prioritization and deconfliction in order to enable UNSOH to respond swiftly to operational demands in Haiti. The Advisory Committee trusts that the roles of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General, as Head of BINUH, and of the Assistant Secretary-General, as Head of UNSOH, will be clearly delineated and that information thereon will be provided in the next budget submission. The Committee also trusts that updated information on the compacts of the Head of UNSOH with both the Special Representative of BINUH and the special representative for the Gang Suppression Force will be provided in the next report.
Trust fund for personnel costs of the Gang Suppression Force

7.	As indicated above, the personnel costs for the Gang Suppression Force are to be funded from voluntary contributions. Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the Department of Operational Support would act as Programme Manager for the trust fund in support of the Multinational Security Support Mission and the Gang Suppression Force. It was also indicated that the oversight of the trust fund, including its management and utilization, were vested with the Department of Operational Support, which would provide the full range of administrative and financial support, and that information on the status of the fund would be shared with UNSOH. The Committee was informed that an amount of $203.3 million had so far been pledged for the trust fund. An amount of $173.6 million had been received, and $55.3 million had been disbursed, resulting in a cash balance of $118.4 million, excluding residual costs related to the Multinational Security Support Mission, and with continuing agreed monthly commitments for the Gang Suppression Force. At the full strength of the envisaged 5,500 uniformed personnel, the personnel reimbursements originating from the trust fund would amount to approximately $100 million annually. The Advisory Committee trusts that the Secretariat will continue to support the mobilization of the required resources for the operationalization of the Gang Suppression Force and that the responsibilities and oversight mechanism, including audit reviews, of the trust fund will be clearly defined. The Committee trusts that updates on the status of voluntary contributions will be provided in the next budget submission (see also A/80/567, para. 9). The Committee further trusts that more detailed information on amounts cost-recovered by the Organization to provide administrative and financial support to the trust fund will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of consideration of the present report and in the next report.
Impact of the UN80 Initiative

8.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, cognizant of the liquidity situation for peacekeeping operations, the requirements included in the proposed budgets for UNSOH for 2025/26 and for 2026/27 had reflected a prudent estimate of the required resources. The Committee was also informed that the liquidity constraints had not yet impacted the operations of UNSOH in its start-up phase but that the liquidity situation would guide the release of allotments for UNSOH in the 2026/27 period. The Advisory Committee notes that UNSOH has received a full allotment for the 2025/26 period. The Committee notes that the information on the proposed resources for 2026/27 does not include efforts for a more efficient delivery of the mandate in the context of the UN80 Initiative and trusts that more detailed information on the ways in which the spirit and goals of the Initiative have been applied to the UNSOH budget proposal for 2026/27 will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.

text: III.	Proposed budget for the period from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026

children.text: A.	Resource requirements
Financial resources – (Thousands of United States dollars)

table_content.text: Commitment authority as per General Assembly resolution 80/241
Commitment authority as per General Assembly resolution 80/241

children.text: a	An amount of $270,004,500 has been assessed on Member States, including commitments of $54,163,500 (plus the equivalent of $50 million from strategic deployment stocks) for the period 1 October to 31 December 2025 under the authority of the Advisory Committee (AC 2358).

b	The total expenditure of $92,096,300 comprises: (a) $65,842,737 from the funds provided under the commitment authority (see General Assembly resolution 80/241); and (b) an equivalent of $26,394,763 in equipment and materials received from strategic deployment stocks.

9.	The proposed requirements for UNSOH for 2025/26 amount to $271,328,700, equal to the amount provided under a commitment authorized by the General Assembly in its resolution 80/241. The requirements for nine months of the 2025/26 period, from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026, are based on deploying and providing support to 1,500 military contingent personnel and 140 formed police unit personnel starting on 1 April 2026 and on deploying an additional 1,860 contingent personnel by 1 July 2026. According to the Secretary-General, the requirements are based on high-level indicative estimates, taking into consideration peacekeeping and support office experience; average costs across medium-sized and large peacekeeping missions; previous peacekeeping missions in Haiti; and the United Nations Support Office in Somalia (UNSOS), adjusted to take into consideration the different operating environment in Haiti (see A/80/860, paras. 10 and 16; and paras. 17–19 below). Information provided to the Advisory Committee indicates that the estimated cost-recovery revenue for 2025/26 would amount to $1,432,000 (see also para. 60 below).

10.	The Advisory Committee notes that, in allocating the 10 per cent adjustment (or $30,147,600) approved by the General Assembly in its resolution 80/241, the proposed budget for 2025/26 includes a 27.7 per cent reduction in the estimated amounts for group I, a 24.9 per cent reduction in the estimated amounts for group II and a 3.4 per cent reduction in the estimated amounts for group III, compared with the initial estimated amounts proposed for the commitment authority by the Secretary-General. As at 31 March 2026, expenditure amounted to $92,096,300, inclusive of $26,394,800 in equipment and materials received from strategic deployment stocks. According to the Secretariat, at the end of the current financial period, estimated total expenditure would amount to $270,815,700, with an unencumbered balance of $513,000 (0.2 per cent) and a resource utilization rate of 99.8 per cent. As at 31 March 2026, the allotment received by UNSOH for 2025/26 was $271,328,700, which is the full amount of the aforementioned commitment authorized by the General Assembly in its resolution 80/241.

11.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the Secretariat anticipated that the full commitment authority in the amount of $271,328,700 would be expended, as the rate of expenditure would increase leading up to 1 April 2026 and thereafter. A number of activities were expected to occur during the remainder of the 2025/26 period, including the conclusion of significant contracts for the turnkey level-2 medical facility in Port-au-Prince, a second ballistic-protected civilian helicopter, the guaranteed availability of an air ambulance and construction contracts. Also, additional personnel of the Gang Suppression Force were expected to be deployed during 2025/26, and UNSOH would therefore incur material costs in the airlift of personnel, as well as airlift or sealift of contingent-owned equipment from countries geographically remote from Haiti. The first tranche of equipment from the strategic deployment stocks had arrived in early March 2026. Also, 87 personnel had been recruited.

12.	While acknowledging that the rate of implementation is expected to progress in a growing trajectory over the reporting period, the Advisory Committee is of the view that the already indicated delays in the deployment of uniformed personnel in comparison to the previous estimates (see para. 4 above), as well as the challenges in the context of operations, may impact the resource utilization for the 2025/26 period. The Committee trusts that updated information on expenditures and variances in comparison to the proposed budget, as well as any related challenges experienced by UNSOH, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the performance report.

13.	The Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 16 February 2026, a total of $270,004,500, had been assessed on Member States in respect of UNSOH since its inception. Payments received as at the same date amounted to $100,412,800, leaving an outstanding balance of $169,591,700. As at 30 March 2026, the cash available to the mission amounted to $128,938,042, which was sufficient to cover the three-month operating reserve of $88,856,000. No claims or reimbursements to troop- and police-contributing countries have been incurred.
1.	Military and police personnel
Delayed deployment factors – (Percentage of approved strength)
a	12-month adjusted vacancy rate.

14.	The proposed resources for military and police personnel for the period from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 amount to $40,428,400 to support the deployment of the Gang Suppression Force of 3,360 military contingent personnel (1,500 by 1 April 2026 and 1,860 by 1 July 2026) and 140 formed police unit personnel, for their travel on emplacement, rotation and repatriation; rations; contingent-owned equipment reimbursement; and freight and deployment of contingent-owned equipment.

15.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the proposed vacancy rates for the Gang Suppression Force of 93.2 per cent for military contingent personnel and 94.3 per cent for formed police unit personnel for 2025/26 had been based on a proposed average deployment of 337 military contingent personnel and 32 formed police unit personnel per month.
2.	Civilian personnel
a	Funded under the commitment authorized by the General Assembly in its resolution 80/241.

16.	The proposed resources for civilian personnel for the 2025/26 period amount to $18,489,500 for 264 international staff (1 Assistant Secretary-General, 1 D-2, 3 D-1, 16 P-5, 40 P-4, 57 P-3, 7 P-2 and 139 Field Service); 23 National Professional Officers; and 132 national General Service staff, with the application of a vacancy rate of 75 per cent for all categories for their salaries, staff assessment and common staff costs. The requirements also cover danger pay of 236 international staff, 19 National Professional Officers and 117 national General Service staff based in Port-au-Prince. The requirements take into account an amount of $1,432,000 to be recovered from BINUH for mission support services provided by UNSOH by all staff categories. The Advisory Committee addresses both periods in the paragraphs below, as appropriate.
United Nations Support Office in Haiti staffing complement assumptions

17.	As indicated above, the Secretary-General has based the resource assumptions, including regarding the staffing complement, on the experience gained in the context of other peacekeeping and support offices, including UNSOS, with appropriate adjustments to take into consideration the different operating environment in Haiti. Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the structure of UNSOS had been used as a baseline for the UNSOH staffing complement throughout the planning process. The Committee was informed that the UNSOS structure had been adjusted to reflect the specific operational conditions of UNSOH, including a smaller supported force, a more centralized geographic footprint around Port-au-Prince and a threat environment shaped by urban gang violence rather than asymmetric conflict, as is the case in the UNSOS area of operations. These factors had resulted in proportionally fewer engineering, transport and aviation posts, the consolidation of supply chain and asset management functions and a shift in security staffing, with a focus on close protection, access control and analysis. Also, some elements of the UNSOS model had been excluded or reduced, including those that were not considered applicable to Haiti and its area of operations, such as sector-based support hubs, large engineering contingents, extensive aviation support structures and specialized security functions. The Advisory Committee notes differences in the scale of operations to be supported by each Support Office, as indicated in the table below, and trusts that more information on the correlation between the size of each Support Office and the scale of operations supported will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.
Comparison between the United Nations Support Office in Haiti and the United Nations Support Office in Somalia

table_content.text: United Nations Support Office in Haiti
United Nations Support Office in Haiti
United Nations Support Office in Somaliaa
United Nations Support Office in Somaliaa

children.text: 18.	As noted in the table above, 419 UNSOH civilian staff would support 5,500 uniformed personnel of the Gang Suppression Force, resulting in a ratio of 1 civilian staff to 13 uniformed personnel, and would provide support services to BINUH and, when required, in-theatre medical evacuation for the Haitian National Police and Haitian Armed Forces (see para. 2 above). From the information provided to it, the Advisory Committee notes that a total of 50 non-United Nations civilian staff would be provided under the umbrella of the Gang Suppression Force. The Advisory Committee trusts that more information on the planned civilian complement of the Gang Suppression Force will be provided in the next report.

19.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that all of the 419 posts are proposed for establishment in the 2025/26 period and that no new posts are proposed for establishment in the 2026/27 period. The civilian staffing requirements for UNSOH, covering both the 2025/26 and 2026/27 periods, are described in section E of the report of the Secretary-General.

20.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 31 March 2026, the selection for 166 positions had been finalized and 87 (1 Assistant Secretary-General, 1 D-2, 4 P-5, 3 P-4, 10 P-3, 1 P-2, 31 Field Service, 3 National Professional Officer, 33 national General Service) positions had been filled. The Committee notes that, out of the 87 positions encumbered, 36 were national posts, representing 41 per cent of the recruited staff. According to the Secretariat, the recruitment of UNSOH staff for the 2025/26 period would be phased as in the table below.
United Nations Support Office in Haiti civilian staff recruitment schedule and incumbency for the 2025/26 period (as at 31 March 2026) – (Number of civilian staff)

table_content.text: Number of positions for which the selection is finalized (cumulative)

children.text: 21.	From the information provided to it, the Advisory Committee notes that no staff have been recruited as at 31 March 2026 for the following 13 areas: Conduct and Discipline Team; Board of Inquiry; Public Information Unit; Human Rights Due Diligence Policy Unit; Claims Unit; Environmental Unit; Audit Response Unit; Welfare Unit; Training Unit; Gender Affairs Unit; Office of the Chief of Service Delivery; Life Support Section; and Office of the Chief of Supply Chain Management. The Committee also notes that the following mission support units have very few staff compared to the proposed staffing numbers: Field Technology Section (2 out of 25 proposed staff); Engineering and Facilities Management Section (4 out of 36 proposed staff); Supply Chain Acquisition and Performance Management Section (3 out of 31 proposed staff); Movement Control Section (3 out of the 25 proposed staff); and Centralized Warehouse Section (1 out of the 26 proposed staff).

22.	Regarding the recruitment in both financial periods and the proposed application of a 75 per cent vacancy rate for 2025/26 and a 30 per cent vacancy rate for 2026/27, the Advisory Committee was informed, upon enquiry, that a phased approach had been applied to ensure that the right personnel were deployed at the right time to Port-au-Prince and Santo Domingo. According to the Secretariat, a 30 per cent vacancy rate applied to a staffing complement of 419 posts, as proposed for the 2026/27 period, amounted to an average monthly strength of 293 civilian personnel. The Advisory Committee considers that the high projected vacancy factors of 75 per cent in 2025/26 and 30 per cent in 2026/27 reflect the expectation of a phased recruitment, including an average of 293 civilian staff for the 2026/27 period. In view of the evolving situation during the start-up phase of UNSOH, and in the context of the UN80 Initiative, the Committee considers that a conservative and phased approach should be taken to the establishment of posts, complemented by the application of realistic vacancy rates. Furthermore, as important elements of the Gang Suppression Force scale of operations are still to be determined, such as the number of locations and military bases to be supported, a phased establishment would allow for a more careful review of additional post requirements based on workload indicators. The Committee trusts that such information will be presented in the proposed budget for the 2027/28 period, with due regard for the spirit and goals of the UN80 Initiative (see also A/80/567, para. 17).

23.	Taking into consideration the aforementioned observations, noting that the proposed establishment of posts does not rely on individual post justifications and that the recruitment of staff is progressing at a slower pace than originally intended, and in view of the delayed deployment of the Gang Suppression Force (see paras. 4, 12 and 22 above), the Advisory Committee recommends that the proposed staffing complement for international posts be reduced by 10 per cent at this stage, with the staffing table to be reconfigured accordingly by UNSOH following the decision by the General Assembly and in the light of the evolving situation in the area of operations. Any related operational costs should be adjusted accordingly.
24.	In addition to its recommendation above, the Advisory Committee also makes observations and further recommendations on the civilian personnel requirements in the paragraphs below.

25.	It is proposed to establish one post of Senior Conduct and Discipline Officer (P-5) in the Conduct and Discipline Team, in addition to one Conduct and Discipline Officer (P-3) and one Associate Conduct and Discipline Officer (National Professional Officer) post, to provide policy guidance and technical advice and to handle misconduct cases and conduct training for United Nations civilian personnel-related matters, including on the prevention of sexual exploitation and abuse. By comparison, the Conduct and Discipline Team at UNSOS comprises two Conduct and Discipline Officer (1 P-4, 1 P-3) posts. Noting the efforts under the UN80 Initiative and taking into account that the UNSOS Conduct and Discipline Team is responsible for a larger scale and volume of operations and is headed at the P-4 level, the Advisory Committee recommends to downgrade, at this stage, the Senior Conduct and Discipline Officer (P-5) post to a Conduct and Discipline Officer (P-4) post. The Committee underscores the importance of ensuring the highest standards of conduct in the operations of the Gang Suppression Force and trusts that the Conduct and Discipline Team will undertake a robust risk assessment in the conduct of its work to ensure its support to the necessary support to the Force.

26.	It is proposed to establish a Human Rights Due Diligence Policy Unit comprising seven posts (1 P-5 Senior Human Rights Officer; 6 Human Rights Officers (1 P-4, 1 P-3, 1 P-2, 3 National Professional Officer)). The Advisory Committee notes that the proposed seven posts would support 5,500 uniformed personnel of the Gang Suppression Force while, by comparison, nine posts (1 P-5, 2 P-3 and 6 National Professional Officer) are proposed for establishment in the same Unit at UNSOS to support 11,186 AUSSOM personnel (see also above). Upon enquiry, the Committee was informed that the implementation of the human rights due diligence policy is expected to pose challenges, stemming from the highly volatile and unpredictable security and operational context on the ground and from the complex nature of the Gang Suppression Force and its mandate, compounded by the additional operational complexity of conducting joint operations with the Haitian National Police and the Haitian Armed Forces. The Committee notes, however, that no detailed workload information has been provided to support the requirements of the Unit at UNSOH. The Committee also notes that BINUH has a Human Rights Service composed of 12 posts and positions and headed at the D-1 level. In the absence of measurable workload information, and taking into consideration the difference in the scale of operations supported by UNSOH compared with those of UNSOS, the Advisory Committee recommends against the establishment of the Senior Human Rights Officer (P-5) post at this stage (see also A/80/604/Add.7, para. 33). Any related operational costs should be adjusted accordingly. The Committee trusts that further justification for the staffing complement of the UNSOH Human Rights Due Diligence Policy Unit, including detailed workload information, as well as information on the planned coordination and cooperation with BINUH for increased synergies and reduction of possible overlaps and duplication, will be provided in the budget proposal for the 2027/28 period.

27.	It is proposed to establish, in Santo Domingo, two Field Service posts (1 Finance and Budget Assistant in the Finance and Budget Section; and 1 Facilities Management Assistant in the Engineering and Facilities Management Section). Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the Finance and Budget Section would comprise 15 staff, with 11 staff to be located in Santo Domingo (1 P-4 (vacant), 1 P-3 (vacant), 5 Field Service (2 vacant, 2 encumbered), 1 National Professional Officer (vacant); 3 national General Service (vacant)); and the remaining 4 to be located in Port-au-Prince. The Engineering and Facilities Section would comprise 36 staff, with 2 staff to be located in Santo Domingo (2 Field Service (both vacant), and the remaining 34 staff to be located in Port-au-Prince. Taking into consideration the proposed capacity of international posts in the Finance and Budget Section and in the Engineering and Facilities Management Section, including in Santo Domingo, the Advisory Committee recalls paragraph 34 of General Assembly resolution 76/274, recommends against the establishment of the aforementioned Field Service posts in Santo Domingo and recommends instead their establishment as National Professional Officer posts.

28.	The Advisory Committee trusts that the staffing proposals for the 2027/28 period will provide more detailed workload information, comparing the proposed configuration of mission support structures with the scale and complexity of operations of the Gang Suppression Force (see para. 11 above) and will reflect the evolution of the operational situation on ground and the deployment pace of the Force. The Committee also acknowledges that staff hired by UNSOH to date have included an important component of national staff and reiterates that efforts should be made to increase the proportion of national staff as early as possible and as UNSOH matures, including through the development of appropriate measures for capacity-building and on-the-job training of national staff (see also A/80/567, para. 16, and General Assembly resolution 76/274, para. 34).
Recruitment

29.	The Advisory Committee was informed that, for the 2025/26 period, all positions had been issued as temporary job openings, as they were funded under the commitment authority until 30 June 2026. Subject to the approval of the proposed staffing table for the 2026/27 period, a new recruitment exercise would be initiated in July 2026, with the posts to be funded for 12 months and subject to the standard recruitment process. The Committee was also informed that, to date, a total of 76 staff had been recruited from the pool of candidates from downsizing entities who received priority consideration, and that one external candidate had been selected (see also A/80/567, para. 19).

30.	The Advisory Committee trusts that internal and external applicants will be treated equally during the recruitment process and that information on the recruitment status of UNSOH posts at all levels, including those recruited in accordance with the downsizing policy in the context of the UN80 Initiative and the revised estimates for 2026, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report (see also A/80/567, para. 19) and in the budget submission for the 2027/28 period.
Vacancy factors

31.	Regarding the vacancy factors for both financial periods, the Secretary-General indicates that: (a) the vacancy rate for the 2025/26 period is based on a 50 per cent vacancy rate and the deployment of staff for 6 months, resulting in a 12-month adjusted vacancy rate of 75 per cent for civilian personnel; and (b) the vacancy rate for the 2026/27 period of 30 per cent is based on the estimated deployment of staff in view of the current pace of recruitment, as in the table below.
Vacancy factors – (Percentage)
a	12-month adjusted vacancy rate.
32.	Subject to its recommendations in paragraphs 22 to 28 above, the Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for civilian personnel.
3.	Operational costs – (Thousands of United States dollars)
a	Funded under the commitment authorized by the General Assembly in its resolution 80/241.

33.	The proposed resources for operational costs for the period from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 amount to $212,410,800, with expenditures as at 31 March 2026 amounting to $72,466,300. Detailed information on the requirements is provided in section III of the report of the Secretary-General.

34.	The Advisory Committee trusts that the Secretary-General will provide to the General Assembly, at the time of its consideration of the present report, the most recent expenditure information for operational costs for the 2025/26 period, including on those budget lines that are considered one-time and non-recurring costs and are related to the start-up phase of UNSOH. Such information should also be included in the performance report for the 2025/26 period.
Facilities and Infrastructure
35.	The proposed resources for facilities and infrastructure for the period from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 amount to $121,787,800, with expenditures as at 31 March 2026 amounting to $40,799,100.

36.	Supplementary information provided to the Advisory Committee indicates that, from inception in the 2025/26 period throughout 2026/27 period, an amount of $140,818,000 is proposed for 34 major construction projects (27 multi-year, 7 single-year), as follows: (a) $54,400,000 for the 2025/26 period; and (b) $86,418,000 for the 2026/27 period. Upon enquiry, the Committee was informed that most sections and units would be located in Port-au-Prince, while three sections (Finance and Budget Section; Human Resources Section; and Procurement Section) would be located in Santo Domingo, where UNSOH had entered into a lease arrangement for premises previously occupied by the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees. The Committee was also informed that, during 2025/26, UNSOH projects had started, with the enhancement of security for the compounds in Port-au-Prince and the establishment of a temporary operating based at the seaport. The Committee was informed that a memorandum of understanding between UNSOH and the Government of Haiti had been signed on 15 December 2025 for the use of land at the airport. Civilian staff had been temporarily accommodated in a leased hotel, with lease agreements for more hotels under negotiation to house Gang Suppression Force staff. In addition, the uniformed personnel of the Gang Suppression Force would be accommodated in a former United Nations Mission for Justice Support in Haiti location, which was being rehabilitated by UNSOH. The Committee was informed that, as contracts were executed, expenditures would be expected to increase given the scale of the works, and that UNSOH anticipated that the budgeted requirements would be fully expended during the two financial periods. The Advisory Committee trusts that more detailed information on the accommodations of all personnel deployed by UNSOH, including leases and related costs, as appropriate, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next report.
37.	The Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for operational costs.

text: IV.	Proposed budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027

children.text: A.	Resource requirements
Financial resources – (Thousands of United States dollars)

table_content.text: Commitment authority as per General Assembly resolution 80/241

children.text: a	An amount of $270,004,500 has been assessed on Member States, including commitments of $54,163,500 (plus the equivalent of $50 million from strategic deployment stocks) for the period 1 October to 31 December 2025 under the authority of the Advisory Committee (AC 2358).

b	The total expenditure of $92,096,300 comprises: (a) $65,842,737 from the funds provided under the commitment authority (see General Assembly resolution 80/241); and (b) an equivalent of $26,394,763 in equipment and materials received from strategic deployment stocks.

38.	The proposed resources amount to $403,583,200 for 2026/27, an increase of $132,254,500, or 48.7 per cent, compared with the amount of $271,328,700 proposed for 2025/26. As at 31 March 2026, expenditure amounted to $92,096,300 from the resources provided under the commitment authority.
1.	Military and police personnel
Delayed deployment factors – (Percentage of approved strength)
a	12-month adjusted vacancy rate.

39.	The proposed requirements for the support to be provided to the Gang Suppression Force for 2026/27 amount to $94,628,100, reflecting an increase of $54,199,700, or 134.1 per cent, compared with the amount of $40,428,400 proposed for 2025/26. As at 31 March 2026, expenditure amounted to $17,803,300 under the resources provided under the commitment authority. The increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) an increase in troops from 1,500 in 2025/26 to 3,360 as of 1 July 2026 and 4,940 as of 1 January 2027, and of formed police unit personnel from 140 in 2025/26 to 560 as of 1 January 2027; (b) the provision of rations for 12 months in 2026/27 instead of 3 months in 2025/26; (c) the reimbursement of contingent-owned equipment for 12 months in 2026/27, instead of 3 months in 2025/26; and (d) the first rotation of 3,360 troops deployed in 2025/26, offset in part by lower freight costs due to a reduction in the deployment of contingent-owned equipment (see A/80/630, paras. 70–74).

40.	The Advisory Committee notes that the requirements for the 2025/26 period are planned to support an average deployment of 1,500 uniformed personnel for a period of 3 months and that the requirements for the 2026/27 period would fund an average deployment of 4,000 uniformed personnel for 12 months which, without other variance factors, would represent a 967 per cent increase in costs, while the proposed requirements have increased by 134.1 per cent compared with 2025/26. Cognizant that the projected variances in the proposal under group I reflect factors other than the increase in the average number of deployed uniformed personnel to be supported by UNSOH, the Advisory Committee trusts that further details and a breakdown of the variances between the proposed budgets under group I for the 2025/26 and the 2026/27 periods will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.
41.	The Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for military and police personnel.
2.	Civilian staffing
a	Funded under the commitment authorized by the General Assembly in its resolution 80/241.

42.	The proposed resources for civilian personnel for the 2026/27 period amount to $50,691,700, reflecting an increase of $32,202,200, or 174.2 per cent, compared with the amount of $18,489,500 for 2025/26. The staffing is unchanged compared to 2025/26 and would provide for 264 international staff (1 Assistant Secretary-General, 1 D-2, 3 D-1, 16 P-5, 40 P-4, 57 P-3, 7 P-2 and 139 Field Service); 23 National Professional Officers; and 132 national General Service staff, with the application of a vacancy rate of 30 per cent for all staff categories, for their salaries, staff assessment, and common staff costs. The Advisory Committee trusts that the Secretary-General will provide updated information on the latest actual vacancy rate to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report, along with a clear justification on realistic projected hirings and the impact of the recommendation of the Committee against the proposed establishments, in order to enable a more informed decision by the Assembly.
Recommendations on posts and positions

43.	As indicated above, all 419 UNSOH posts have been proposed for establishment in the 2025/26 period and no further posts have been requested for the 2026/27 period. Subject to its recommendations pertaining to the staffing proposals for the 2025/26 period in paragraphs 22 to 28 above, the Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for civilian personnel. Any related operational costs should be adjusted accordingly.
3.	Operational costs – (Thousands of United States dollars)
a	Funded under the commitment authorized by the General Assembly in its resolution 80/241.

44.	The proposed resources for operational costs for 2026/27 amount to $258,263,400, reflecting an increase of $45,852,600, or 21.6 per cent, compared with the amount of $212,410,800 proposed for 2025/26. The increase reflects higher requirements under consultants and consultancy services, facilities and infrastructure, air operations, and medical, offset in part by lower requirements under official travel, ground transportation, marine operations, communications and information technology, and other supplies, services and equipment (see A/80/630, sects. II and III).
Facilities and infrastructure

45.	The proposed resources for facilities and infrastructure amount to $154,844,400 for 2026/27, representing an increase of $33,056,600, or 27.1 per cent, compared with the proposed level of $121,787,800 for 2025/26. Expenditure amounted to $40,799,100 as at 31 March 2026.

46.	The increased requirements for the 2026/27 period are attributable primarily to: (a) the continued implementation of the multi-year infrastructure project to support 5,500 uniformed and 50 civilian personnel of the Gang Suppression Force and UNSOH civilian personnel, with expansion in the regions and the implementation of a renewable energy project that includes the installation of turnkey solar hybrid systems; and (b) the provision of utilities and waste disposal services, maintenance services, security services, spare parts and supplies, the rental of premises, and fuel for 12 months in the 2026/27 period compared with 6 months in the 2025/26 period, offset in part by the discontinuation of the one-time provision for the acquisition of equipment for the start-up of UNSOH.

47.	Supplementary information provided to the Advisory Committee indicates that, from inception in the 2025/26 period throughout the 2026/27 period, an amount of $140,818,000 is proposed for 34 major construction projects (27 multi-year and 7 single-year, including 4 single-year in the 2026/27 period), as follows: (a) $54,400,000 for the 2025/26 period; and (b) $86,418,000 for the 2025/26 period. In 2026/27, UNSOH plans to set up movement control terminals and various storage areas in Port-au-Prince and the sectors, and initiate multi-year projects, including construction activities in support of the forward operating bases; road improvement works; and other infrastructure in the sectors. The Committee notes that the planning assumptions foresee construction related to 2 additional sectors beyond Port-au-Prince and to 12 forward operating bases.

48.	The Advisory Committee also notes that, while the proposed appropriations under acquisition of engineering supplies; acquisition of prefabricated facilities, accommodation and refrigeration equipment; and acquisition of generators and electrical equipment have expenditures close to or beyond the level of appropriation, the subclasses construction, alteration, renovation and major maintenance; construction materials and field defence supplies; and maintenance services have expenditures, as at 31 March 2026, of $1,745.400, $544,200 and $0 against proposed appropriations of $54,400,000, $9,000,000 and $7,563,900, respectively.

49.	The Advisory Committee notes the low expenditure levels to-date under the budget subclasses under facilities and infrastructure, with the exception of those related to the acquisition of assets, where expenditures are expedited as a result of access to the strategic deployment stocks. Taking into consideration the aforementioned restrained deployment pace of UNSOH civilian staff and of Gang Suppression Force uniformed personnel, as well as the evolving situation in the area of operations and in the concept of operations of the Force, the Committee considers that the planned work on various projects for the 2026/27 period may face delays and would affect other related requirements under facilities and infrastructure, such as maintenance services. The Committee therefore recommends a reduction of 5 per cent ($7,742,200) to the proposed resources for facilities and infrastructure. The Committee trusts that an update on the expenditures and the status of the construction projects, the rental of premises and the establishment of main contracts will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the budget submission for the 2027/28 period (see also A/80/567, para. 23).
Air operations

50.	The proposed resources for air operations amount to $38,161,800 for 2026/27, representing an increase of $26,087,100, or 216.0 per cent, compared with the proposed level of $12,074,700 for 2025/26. Expenditure amounted to $1,994,700 as at 31 March 2026.

51.	From the supplementary information provided to it, the Advisory Committee notes that the planning assumptions under air operations foresee the deployment of a fleet of 13 aircraft, including 11 rotary-wing, 2 civilian and 9 military. Upon enquiry, the Committee was informed that aviation support would be provided through the deployment of two guaranteed-availability civilian medium utility rotary-wing aircraft. The first was positioned in Port-au-Prince on 12 January 2026 and the second would be positioned by mid-March. In 2026/27, one fixed-wing passenger aircraft would be deployed, supplemented by the use of on-call air arrangements. In addition, five helicopters were currently deployed to the Gang Suppression Force. Upon enquiry, the Committee was also informed that negotiations for the deployment of an additional four military rotary-wing aircraft were being finalized, with full operational capability expected by June 2026, and that these assets had been budgeted for 12 months in the 2026/27 period. The Advisory Committee trusts that an update on the status of the deployment of helicopters will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report, and that the related requirements for the 2027/28 period will be adjusted accordingly, as appropriate.
Communications and information technology

52.	The proposed resources for communications and information technology amount to $14,836,200 for 2026/27, representing a decrease of $5,506,600, or 27.1 per cent, compared with the proposed level of $20,342,800 for 2025/26. Expenditure amounted to $4,398,100 as at 31 March 2026.

53.	The reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the discontinuation of the one-time provision for the acquisition and installation of equipment for the start-up of UNSOH, offset in part by increased requirements for communication services and the rental of an increased number of telecommunications towers and microwave hubs as a result of the increase in the number of troops and the number of locations. Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the reduced requirements reflected the completion of the initial infrastructure for UNSOH and the headquarters of the Gang Suppression Force and covered the regional expansion in two regions, in line with the phased deployment of the Force by January 2027. The Committee was informed that the requirements for recurring services for telecommunications services amounted to $4,770,400 in 2026/27, representing an increase of $1,699,400 compared to $3,071,000 in 2025/26. Taking into consideration the restrained deployment of UNSOH civilian staff and of Gang Suppression Force personnel, as well as the evolving situation in the area of operations, the Advisory Committee considers that the expansion to other regions and installation of a range of equipment, and the use of related communications and information technology services, may not be implemented as planned and therefore recommends a reduction of 5 per cent ($741,800) to the proposed resources for communications and information technology. The Committee trusts that an update on the most recent expenditures will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.
54.	Subject to its recommendations in paragraphs 49 and 53 above, the Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for operational costs.
United Nations regulatory frameworks and the Gang Suppression Force

55.	In its resolution 2793 (2025), the Security Council requested the Member States participating in the Gang Suppression Force to ensure the highest standards of transparency, conduct and discipline for their contingents operating in the framework of the Gang Suppression Force and set detailed requirements for the establishment, among others, of a robust compliance mechanism; an oversight mechanism to prevent human rights violations or abuses; of mechanisms to detect incidents and ensure a safe survivor-and-victim centred response in cases of misconduct involving their personnel, including by providing safe and accessible complaint and referral mechanisms and conducting timely investigations of all allegations of misconduct, holding perpetrators accountable and repatriating units when there is credible evidence of misconduct, including widespread or systemic sexual exploitation and abuse by those units of the Gang Suppression Force. The Advisory Committee recalls that, as a non-United Nations mission, United Nations oversight and accountability mechanisms would not apply to the Gang Suppression Force, with the exception of the United Nations human rights due diligence policy. Furthermore, the special representative for the Gang Suppression Force would need to establish capacities within their office with regard to sexual exploitation and abuse involving members of the Gang Suppression Force, including a board of inquiry and other essential oversight mechanisms, in line with resolution 2793 (2025) (see also A/80/567, para. 7).

56.	The Advisory Committee notes the detailed requirements contained in Security Council resolution 2793 (2025) regarding compliance with the human rights due diligence policy and the establishment of mechanisms to ensure the highest standards of transparency, conduct and discipline for contingents operating in the framework of the Gang Suppression Force. The Committee trusts that further information on the aforementioned matters will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget report, including on the setting of the mechanisms required by resolution 2793 (2025) (ibid., paras. 7–8).
Strategic deployment stocks

57.	In the report, it is indicated that three tranches of equipment from the strategic deployment stocks were scheduled to arrive in Port-au-Prince by the end of March 2026, with additional shipments planned during the 2026/27 period. Shipments also include assets from closing and downsizing missions, which are considered for transfer to UNSOH based on needs and must be in good condition to ensure that their value and remaining useful life is worth the cost of transfer and associated shipping. In addition, a range of equipment and supplies for the UNSOH close protection team would be transferred from the United Nations Assistance Mission in Iraq during the 2025/26 period (see also A/80/630, para. 26).

58.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 31 March 2026, an equivalent value of $26,394,763 had been transferred to UNSOH from strategic deployment stocks and that the related replenishment of the stocks at the United Nations Logistics Base at Brindisi, Italy was under way. The Committee was informed that 60 sea containers had been procured in 2025/26 and that an additional 23 containers were expected to be acquired in 2026/27. In addition, all equipment and supplies shipped or scheduled to be shipped from strategic deployment stocks are shipped in containers from these stocks, while shipments from other missions are shipped in United Nations reserve containers, which are containers received from liquidating missions.

59.	The Advisory Committee trusts that an update on assets and the related value received from strategic deployment stocks, as well as assets received from other internal sourcing options, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the performance report for the 2025/26 period (see also A/80/567, paras. 26–28). The Advisory Committee discusses strategic deployments stocks in its report on the budget performance for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and the budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 for the United Nations Logistics Base (A/80/604/Add.5, paras. 38–41 and 46).
Support services

60.	Information provided to the Advisory Committee indicates that the estimated cost-recovery revenue amounting to $1,432,000 for 2025/26 and $4,009,600 for 2026/27 has been deducted from the overall staffing costs related to support provided to BINUH. As the support provided by UNSOH to BINUH is on a cost-recovery basis, no financial resources for the support to BINUH have been included in the 2025/26 and 2026/27 UNSOH budget proposals. The Advisory Committee trusts that detailed information on cost-recovery revenue, as well as on the number and level of posts estimates to support BINUH, will be included in the performance report for the 2025/26 period, as well as in future budget reports.

61.	The Advisory Committee was informed that, in light of the significant level of work in establishing UNSOH and the dedicated human resources capacity required, it had been determined that UNSOH would develop an internal capacity to carry out transactional work for the surge period. At the appropriate time, the services provided by the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe would be utilized, in line with the standard practice at other field missions. The Advisory Committee trusts that information on the expected timeline to conclude surge start-up requirements and plans for the transfer of support services to the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe will be included in the budget submission for the 2027/28 period.
Environmental initiatives

62.	In paragraph 19 of its resolution 2793 (2025), the Security Council called upon Member States participating in the Gang Suppression Force to adopt appropriate water and wastewater management and other environmental controls to guard against the introduction and spread of waterborne diseases, in cooperation with the Haitian authorities. The report of the Secretary-General indicates that related risk prevention and mitigation measures have been integrated into UNSOH planning and operations, including wastewater treatment plants and waste management yards at all main UNSOH bases and at the Gang Suppression Force headquarters. Also, the Department of Operational Support and UNSOH have jointly developed initial energy, waste and wastewater management plans, with best practices incorporated. The Advisory Committee was also informed that the Department of Operational Support environment strategy for peace operations, promulgated in 2017, now includes UNSOH as the first large start-up operation as part of the Department’s environmental risk and performance framework.

63.	The Advisory Committee encourages UNSOH to continue undertaking all efforts to ensure that the appropriate environmental safeguards are established from the very beginning of its operations. The Committee trusts that further information thereon, including on any efficiency gains from the environmental initiatives, will be reflected in future budget reports. The Committee also trusts that detailed information on the investments made to advance the environmental initiatives of UNSOH will be included in future budget reports.
Lessons learned

64.	As indicated in paragraph 16 of the report of the Secretary-General, the requirements and planning assumptions of UNSOH have been based on experience gained in a range of operations and circumstances. The Advisory Committee was informed, upon enquiry, that the lessons learned applied for the start-up and establishment of UNSOH pertained to procurement and financial management, as well as a phased recruitment approach, environmental management and the operationalization of the integrated supply chain project, jointly led by the Department of Operational Support, the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) and the World Food Programme, aimed at coordinated procurement, global logistics and in-country logistics harmonization. All UNSOH start-up lessons learned would be documented in order to support the operationalization of future new field operations. The Advisory Committee trusts that information on lessons learned from the UNSOH startup phase will be provided in the next report. Furthermore, the Committee trusts that detailed information on the implementation of the integrated supply chain project in Haiti in cooperation with other entities, the role of UNSOH and the application of the Secretariat regulatory framework in that context, as well as any related efficiencies, will be provided in future reports.
Equitable geographical representation and gender parity

65.	As UNSOH is currently in its start-up phase, information on the geographical representation and gender of staff is not yet available. The Advisory Committee trusts that UNSOH will undertake efforts to promote equitable geographical representation and promote gender parity when recruiting personnel (see also A/80/567, para. 19). The Advisory Committee discusses equitable geographical representation and gender parity in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).

66.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly with regard to the financing of the United Nations Support Office in Haiti for the periods from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 and from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 are indicated in section IV of the report of the Secretary-General (A/80/630).

67.	Taking into account its observations and recommendations above, the Advisory Committee recommends that the proposed resources be reduced by $1,951,000, from $271,328,700 to $269,377,700. Accordingly, the Committee recommends that the General Assembly appropriate the amount of $269,377,700 for the maintenance of the Mission for the nine-month period from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026.

68.	Taking into account its observations and recommendations above, the Advisory Committee recommends that the proposed resources be reduced by $13,814,400, from $403,583,200 to $389,768,800. Accordingly, the Committee recommends that the General Assembly appropriate the amount of $389,768,800 for the maintenance of the Mission for the 12-month period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027.

69.	A summary of the budgetary impact and the financial implications resulting from the recommendations of the Advisory Committee with regard to the financing of UNSOH for the periods from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 and from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 is presented in the table below.
